nativeRange.habitat: Clear, fast-flowing limestone springs and spring-fed streams in the Maros district of South Sulawesi; hard, alkaline water with dense riparian vegetation and rocky substrates

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Vallisneria, Ceratopteris, floating plants)
gentle current via sponge or hang-on-back filter

diet.notes: Primarily a small live- and frozen-food enthusiast in the wild; feeds on small insects, zooplankton, and tiny crustaceans. In the aquarium, readily accepts high-quality micro-pellets, finely crushed flake, and small frozen/live foods: daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, and cyclops. Varied diet with live or frozen foods is important for conditioning breeding pairs and maintaining colour intensity. Avoid large foods — the small mouth cannot handle standard-sized flake pieces.

goodWith: Sulawesi shrimp (with caution — match water parameters)
small tetras tolerant of hard alkaline water
avoidWith: soft-water species incompatible with hard alkaline conditions

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Marosatherina ladigesi; shows adult male with extended fin filaments. Verify availability and license before use.
Wikimedia Commons image filed under the former synonym Telmatherina ladigesi; shows a group of adults. Verify current file status.

summary: The Celebes Rainbowfish (Marosatherina ladigesi) is a small, slender fish endemic to limestone springs in the Maros district of South Sulawesi, Indonesia, where it is classified Critically Endangered. Despite the trade name, it belongs to the family Telmatherinidae, not the true rainbowfish family. Males display elongated fin filaments. Requires hard, alkaline water.
careNotes:
The Celebes Rainbowfish (Marosatherina ladigesi) is a small, elegant fish endemic to clear, fast-flowing limestone springs in the Maros district of South Sulawesi, Indonesia. Although it shares the trade name 'rainbowfish,' it belongs to the family Telmatherinidae — a distinct group from the Australian and New Guinean rainbowfishes of family Melanotaeniidae — and is sometimes still listed in older literature under the synonym Telmatherina ladigesi. It is classified Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List due to habitat loss and degradation within its extremely restricted range.

Water chemistry is a non-negotiable aspect of care: the species originates in hard, alkaline limestone-spring water (pH 7.5–8.5, gH 10–20) and will not thrive in the soft, acidic conditions suitable for many popular community fish. Keeping it in soft water increases susceptibility to bacterial and fungal infections. If using reverse-osmosis water, it should be remineralised to reach the appropriate hardness; a small amount of coral sand or crushed coral in the filter can help buffer pH upward.

Keep in groups of at least 6 (more is better) to reduce individual timidity and encourage natural shoaling and display behaviour. Males spar vigorously with extended fin filaments but rarely cause injury; providing multiple females per male (a 2–3:1 ratio) distributes spawning attention. The aquarium should be mature, well-filtered but gentle (the species dislikes strong turbulence), and heavily planted. A covered tank is advisable — this species is an occasional jumper. Temperature should be maintained at 24–28°C; prolonged cold spells below 22°C cause stress and weaken the immune system.
breedingNotes: Moderate breeding difficulty. Celebes Rainbowfish are egg scatterers that spawn among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops; eggs are relatively large and adhesive. Condition breeding groups with varied live and frozen foods (daphnia, baby brine shrimp) for 1–2 weeks before attempting a breeding setup. Transfer a conditioned pair or trio to a dedicated 30–40 L breeding tank densely planted with fine-leaved plants (Cabomba, Java moss, spawning mops) at the same water parameters. Adults will eat eggs and fry; remove adults after spawning, or use a breeding trap/mesh. Eggs hatch in approximately 7–10 days at 26°C. Feed infusoria or commercial first-fry foods initially, transitioning to micro-worms and baby brine shrimp nauplii as fry grow. Fry are delicate and water quality must be maintained carefully. Growth is slow.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Marosatherina ladigesi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Telmatherinidae, Atheriniformes), adult size (~7 cm), temperature 24–28°C, pH 7.5–8.5, hardness 10–20 dGH, native range (Maros district, South Sulawesi), habitat (limestone springs), diet (small invertebrates, zooplankton), breeding behaviour (egg-scatterer among plants), and IUCN Critically Endangered status. Also confirmed synonym Telmatherina ladigesi.

additional.name: FishBase — Marosatherina ladigesi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max ~7.5 cm TL), taxonomy (Telmatherinidae, Atheriniformes), native range (Sulawesi, Indonesia), temperature tolerance (24–28°C), diet classification (omnivore/insectivore), and IUCN Critically Endangered status. Confirmed synonym Telmatherina ladigesi (Ahl, 1936).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Celebes Rainbowfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-focused care guide confirming hard alkaline water requirements (pH 7.5–8.5), group keeping (6+), diet (small live/frozen foods and micro-pellets), tank size recommendation (minimum 10 gal / ~38 L for a small group, 20 gal / ~76 L preferred), and note on family distinction from Melanotaeniidae.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Celebes Rainbowfish
additional.notes: UK hobbyist perspective confirming Sulawesi endemic range, Critically Endangered IUCN status, hard alkaline water requirements, male fin filament display behaviour, and note that captive-bred stock is more adaptable than wild-caught but still requires correct pH/hardness parameters.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Marosatherina ladigesi
additional.notes: IUCN Red List entry confirming Critically Endangered (CR) status, restricted range in Maros district karst springs of South Sulawesi, primary threats of habitat modification (agricultural run-off, irrigation extraction, water pollution), and historical overcollection for the aquarium trade. Assessment notes wild population decline.

fish.breedingNotes: The Celebes Rainbowfish is an egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Adults should be removed after spawning to prevent them from eating the eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 7 to 10 days at 26°C. Fry initially require infusoria or a similar first-fry food before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Growth is slow and fry are delicate, so maintaining pristine water quality is essential throughout the rearing period.
